P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Gordon M. Ha rt 
1301 K Street 
Modesto, CA 
95354 
Dear Mr. Hart: 
T ecnnicoJ Assistance 
(916) 322-5662 
Administration 
322..5660 
Executive; Legal 
•• 
Enforcement 
322·5901 
322-6441 
January 6, 1984 
Re: 
Advice Letter No. A-83-264 
Thank you for your request for advice on the conflict of 
interests provisions of tne Political Reform Act. 
The facts, as 
stated in your letter, are that you are a member of the City of 
Modesto Planning Commission. 
You are also the pres'ident and 
.100 percent owner of an architectural and,engineering firm in 
tne City of Modesto. 
Your letter described four situations, and 
you asked how the Act's provisions apply to each: 
Situation #1: 
The City Council hired a Southern California 
architectural firm to provide preliminary studies and, 
eventually, construction drawings and bid documents for a new 
police administration facility. 
Due to your structural 
engineering background, office location in Modesto and knowledge 
of local ouilding procedures and contractors, the Southern 
California arcnitect would 11ke to hire your firm to perform the 
structural engineering services on the police facility. 
You 
stated that there are no zoning or planning matters involved in 
this project. 
You aostained from participating in the review of 
the capital improvement oudget (ClP) in which the allocation of 
money for the project was approved. 
Your question is how the 
will conflict of interests provisions of the Political Reform 
Act apply if you work on the project and are paid by the 
architect. 
S1tuation #2: 
The City Council, acting as a Redevelopment 
Agency, is developing a convention and community center which 
will cost over $50 million. 
Because a recent study suggested 
that sufficient funding for the project may be unavailable, a 
group of local architects, engineers and contractors, including 
your firm, are considering putting together a similar project 
using existing buildings. 
This will provide a less expensive, 


Gordon M. Hart 
January 6, 1984 
Page 2 
out s~milarly functioning, community center. 
If this 
alternative is feasiole, the City will oe asked to fund the 
group's design studies. 
Tne EIR on the City's existing plan was 
reviewed by the Planning Commiss~on while you were a member, out 
all other decisions regarding the project's location and 
property acquisition were made oefore you were a member. 
Your 
question is whether your firm may participate in the feasibility 
and design study and receive a portion of the fee paid to the 
group. 
Situation #3: 
Because of your baCkground in design and 
construction, tne Planning Director and Planning Commission 
cnairman would like you to partic~pate in preparing next year's 
capital improvement plan (CIP). 
The Planning Commission's 
delioerations result in recommendations to the City Council on 
the various items in the CIP. 
Your question ~s whether your 
participation in tne hearings and recommendation process will 
prevent your office from suomitting proposals or being hired by 
the City to.do architectural or engineering work on any of the 
CIP projects eventually adopted oy the City Council. 
Situation #4: 
A client retains your firm to design a 
project that will require zoning or other City planning 
approvals: 
(a) 
Maya representative of your firm make the 
presentation to tne Planning Commission, assuming that you 
disqualify yourself from the decision? 
(0) 
If your associate makes the presentation and there 
is a split vote may you handle the appeal to the City 
Council? 
Situation #5: 
The Board of Zoning Adjustments (BZA) is 
appointed by the City Council and its decisions are ratified by, 
or appealed to, the Council. 
Your question is whether you can 
present zoning or variance requests before the BZA. 
DISCUSSION 
Government Code Section 871001/ pronibits a local public 
official from making, participating in the making, or in any way 
attempting to use his official pOSition to influence a 
1/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the 
Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 


Gordon M. Hart 
January 6, 1984 
Page 3 
governmental dec1sion in whicn he knows or has reason to know he 
has a financial interest. 
An official nas a "financial 
interest" in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if 
it is reasonably foreseeable that tne decision will have a 
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
tne pUblic generally, on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in whicn the public 
offic1al has a direct or indirect investment worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(b) 
Any real property in which tne pUblic 
official has a direct or indirect interest worth more 
than one tnousand dollars ($1,000). 
(c) 
Any source of income • • • • aggregating two 
hundred fifty Qollars ($250) or more in value provided 
to, received oy or promised to tne public official 
within 12 months prior to tne time when tne decision is 
made. 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official is a director, officer •••• or holds any 
position of management. 
(Section 87103.) 
You have an investment interest in Gordon M. Hart, Inc., you 
are an officer 1n the company, and it is a source of income to 
you. 
In addition, because you have a 100 percent interest in 
the firm, any income the firm is deemed to be income to you 
personally. 
(Section 82030(a).) 
Situation 11: 
If you work as a SUbcontractor on the new 
police administration facility, the contractor will become a 
source of income to you once Gordon M. Hart, Inc., is promised, 
or is paid $250 or more in fees. 
Onder the facts provided, the 
City of Modesto will not be a source of income to you. 
You will 
oe required to disqualify yourself from Planning CommisSion 
decisions which will foreseeably have a material financial 
effect on the contractor. 2/ 
The potential for disquali-
fication extends not only to decisions concerning the police 
2/ 
The pnrase "material financial effect" is explained in 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702, copy enclosed. 


Gordon M. Hart 
Janua ry 6, 1984 
Page 4 
administration facility, out to all decisions affecting the 
con tractor. 
Situation #2: 
Your firm, as part of a joint venture, may 
participate in, and oe paid for, designing an alternative to the 
City's planned community center. 
You stated, during a telephone 
conversation, that the Planning Commission will not make or 
participate in any decisions affecting whether the City pays 
for, or adopts, your group's designs.lI 
Situation #3: 
If you decide to participate in the 
preparation of next year's CIP, this will not prevent your firm 
from later working on proJects authorized in the CIP. 
If your 
firm does provide such services, the City will become a source 
of income to you and the discussion contained in footnote 3, 
will Oe applicaole. 
You will be prohibited from making, participating in the 
making, or attempting to use your position to influence 4/ any 
decision concerning a particular project in the CIP if you know. 
or nave reason to know that tne decision will have a material 
financial effect on Gordon M. Hart, Inc., or any of your sources 
of income.21 
(See the enclosed copy of the Thorner opinion, 
3/ 
Assuming that your firm has a 10 percent or greater 
~nterest in the JOint venture, the City will Oecome a source of 
income to you, personally, once it promises or pays sufficient 
fees to the group such that your firm's pro rata share is $250 
or more. 
If the City becomes a source of income to you, you 
will not Oe required to disqualify yourself from decisions 
affecting the City because the City is comprised of the public 
generally and the "public generally" exception will apply. 
(See 
the enclosed copy of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703.) 
You will be required to report the City on your Statement of 
Economic Interests if it pays or becomes obligated to pay 
sufficient fees such that your firm's pro rata share amounts to 
$10,000 or more • 
. i/ The defini tion of "making participating in the making, 
or attempting to use your position to influence" a deCision, is 
contained in tne enclosed copy of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18700. 
"Participating in the making" includes making certain 
types of recommendations. 
~/ If tne City becomes a source of income to you, it is 
exempt for the reason descriOed ~n Situation #2, above. 


Gordon M. Ha rt 
January 6, 1984 
page 5 
No. 75-089, Dec. 4, 1975, which discusses "foreseeability" in 
this context.) 
If you determine that one or more of the items 
containea in tne CIP will require your aisqualification, it is 
possible that you may still Oe allowed to participate in the 
rest of the CIP and you should contact us for more information. 
Situation #4: 
If your firm is hired to design a project 
which will require zoning or other City planning approvals, your 
associate is free to make the presentat~on to the Planning 
Commission. 
However, you must refrain from making, 
participating in or attempting to use your official position to 
influence the decision concerning the approval if the decision 
will foreseeably have a material financial effect on Gordon M. 
Hart, Inc., or one of your sources of income. 
(See 2 Cal. Adm. 
Code Section 18700.) 
"Using your position to influence" a 
decision includes furthering or attempting to affect in any 
manner any decision: 
(1) 
Within or before lyour] ••• agency: or 
(2) 
Before any agency which is appointed by or subject 
to the ouagetary control of [yourJ ••• agency. 
I have included copies of the Harron and Thacher advice letters, 
which discuss this phrase and which should be helpful to you. 
If your firm decides to appeal a Planning Commission 
decision to the City Council, you will be free to appear on your 
clients oehalf. 
You will not De "using your official position 
to influence" the Council's decision oecause the Council is not 
an agency on which you Sit, nor is it an agency that is subject 
to your agency's budgetary or appointive control. 
(See 2 Cal. 
Adm. Code Section 18700 (e) (1) and (2).) 
Situation #5: 
Occasionally your clients have zoning or 
variance requests Defore the BZA. 
The City Council, rather than 
the Planning Commission, has appointive power over the BZA. 
Assuming the Planning Commission does not have budgetary power 
over the Board, you are free to represent your clients before 
the BZA. 
My advice is confined to the provisions of the Political 
Reform Act. 
You should discuss the possible application of 
Government Code Section 1090, or other statutory and common law 
provisions, with your City Attorney or private counsel. 
If you 


Gordon M. Hart 
January 6, 1984 
Page 6 
have any questions concerning the Political Reform Act, please 
feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
JSM:plh 
Enclosures 
Very truly yours, 
:J 
~ ~rJ~CL 
J~hank MCLean-'T~e.t2.n./ 
Counsel 
Legal DiviSion 


GORDON M. HART INC. 
Engineers &. Architects 
Fair Political Practice Commission 
P.o. Box 807 
Sacramento, California 
95804 
Attn: Ms. Barbara Milman 
Chief, Legal Division 
Subject: Requested Legal Opinion 
on Conflict of Interest 
Dear Ms. Milman: 
November 23, 1983 
This letter is a result of a telephone conversation with Bob Leidich 
this morning coneerning potential conflicts of interest. 
In accordance 
with government code 83ll4(d) I am herewith requesting a response to the 
following questions. 
As you can see from our letterhead, I am the president of a consulting 
architectural and engineering firm in the City of Modesto. 
I also serve 
as a member of the City of Modesto Planning Commission. 
I would appre-
ciate a legal opinion on the following four potential conflicts of 
interest. 
1. The City Council has retained a southern California architectural 
firm to provide preliminary studies and eventually will enter into a 
contract to provide construction drawings and bid documents for a new 
police administration facility. 
As a member of the Planning Commis~ 
sion I did not participate in the review of the capital improvement 
budget (CIP) wherein the money for this project was allocated. No 
other zoning or planning items are involved. 
Due to my structural 
engineering background, office location in Modesto and knowledge of 
local building procedures and contractors, the southern California 
architect desires to hire our firm to perform structural engineering 
services on the police facility. 
Question: Does this constitute a conflict of interest due to the fact 
the City of Modesto will be paying the architect who in turn will 
eventually pay me? 
Gordon M. Hart 
Norman E Wilson 
1301 K Street, Modesto, CA 95354 
(209) 521-1 143 


2. The City Council is acting as a Redevelopment Agency for the purpose 
of developing a future $50 million+ convention/community center. 
Although the EIR was reviewed by the Planning Commission, all dec~ 
isions pertaining to location and property acquisition were made 
prior to my appointment. 
A recent study by a consultant suggested a 
negative conclusion concerning potential funding for this project. 
A 
group of local architects, engineers and contractors, including our 
firm, are considering putting together a similar project utilizing 
existing buildings on a similar but identical site thus coming up 
with a considerably less expensive but similarly functioning communi-
ty center. If this potential turns out to be feasible the City will 
be asked to fund the group for these design studies. 
Question: Can our firm participate in this feasibility and design 
study and receive a portion of the fee paid to the group? 
3. As my professional background and experience all involves design and 
construction, the Planning Director and Planning Commission chairman 
desire that I participate in the CIP process next year. 
As you 
probably are aware, deliberations on the CIP only result in a recom-
mendation to the City Council concerning the various items in the 
CIP. 
Question: Does my participation in the hearings and recommendation 
process preclude my office from proposing and/or being hired by the 
City to do architectural or engineering work on any of the CIP pro-
jects eventually adopted by the City Council? 
4. Question: When a client retained my firm to design a project which 
will require zoning or other City planning type approvals, is there 
any prohibition to a representative from my firm, i.e. another archi-
tect or engineer making the presentation to the Commission assuming 
that I step down and do not participate in the decision? 
Correlary Question: Assuming that my associate makes the presentation 
before the Planning Commission (and I have stepped down as noted 
above) and a split vote results and the matter is appealed to the 
City Council, can I personally make this appeal? 
Correlary Question: Do you know of any prohibition that would prevent 
me from personally making zoning or variance type presentations for a 
client before our Board of Zoning Adjustment (BZA)? 
The BZA is 
appointed by the City Council and its decisions are either ratified 
by or can be appealed to the City Council. 


-
I appreciate the opportunity to pose these questions to you and 
apolo~ 
gize for the lengthy dissertation. 
As you can see there is a common 
thread running through all of the questions involving a member of the 
Planning Commission either receiving City funds for City work or making 
presentations for City bodies. 
Thanking you in advance for your consideration. 
Sincerely, 
GORDON M. HART, 
~NC. 
Gordon M. Hart 
President 
GMH/tc

2. The City Council is acting as a Redevelopment Agency for the purpose

Although the EIR was reviewed by the Planning Commission, all dec~ 
isions pertaining to location and property acquisition were made 
prior to my appointment. 
A recent study by a consultant suggested a 
negative conclusion concerning potential funding for this project. 
A 
group of local architects, engineers and contractors, including our 
firm, are considering putting together a similar project utilizing 
existing buildings on a similar but identical site thus coming up 
with a considerably less expensive but similarly functioning communi-
ty center. If this potential turns out to be feasible the City will 
be asked to fund the group for these design studies. 
Question: Can our firm participate in this feasibility and design 
study and receive a portion of the fee paid to the group? 
3. As my professional background and experience all involves design and 
construction, the Planning Director and Planning Commission chairman 
desire that I participate in the CIP process next year. 
As you 
probably are aware, deliberations on the CIP only result in a recom-
mendation to the City Council concerning the various items in the 
CIP. 
Question: Does my participation in the hearings and recommendation 
process preclude my office from proposing and/or being hired by the 
City to do architectural or engineering work on any of the CIP pro-
jects eventually adopted by the City Council? 
4. Question: When a client retained my firm to design a project which 
will require zoning or other City planning type approvals, is there 
any prohibition to a representative from my firm, i.e. another archi-
tect or engineer making the presentation to the Commission assuming 
that I step down and do not participate in the decision? 
Correlary Question: Assuming that my associate makes the presentation 
before the Planning Commission (and I have stepped down as noted 
above) and a split vote results and the matter is appealed to the 
City Council, can I personally make this appeal? 
Correlary Question: Do you know of any prohibition that would prevent 
me from personally making zoning or variance type presentations for a 
client before our Board of Zoning Adjustment (BZA)? 
The BZA is 
appointed by the City Council and its decisions are either ratified 
by or can be appealed to the City Council. 


-
I appreciate the opportunity to pose these questions to you and 
apolo~ 
gize for the lengthy dissertation. 
As you can see there is a common 
thread running through all of the questions involving a member of the 
Planning Commission either receiving City funds for City work or making 
presentations for City bodies. 
Thanking you in advance for your consideration.
sections.conclusion: Situation #1: You will be required to disqualify yourself from Planning Commission decisions which will foreseeably have a material financial effect on the contractor. Situation #2: Your firm, as part of a joint venture, may participate in, and be paid for, designing an alternative to the City's planned community center. Situation #3: Participating in CIP preparation will not prevent your firm from later working on projects authorized in the CIP, but you will be prohibited from influencing decisions concerning particular projects if your firm is interested. Situation #4: Your associate is free to make the presentation to the Planning Commission. You may appear on your client's behalf before the City Council if your firm appeals a Planning Commission decision. Situation #5: You are free to represent your clients before the BZA, assuming the Planning Commission does not have budgetary power over the Board.

sections.question_synthetic: A Planning Commission member who owns an architectural and engineering firm seeks advice on five situations involving potential conflicts of interest under the Political Reform Act: working as a subcontractor on a city project, participating in an alternative development proposal, helping prepare the capital improvement plan, having a firm representative present projects to the Planning Commission, and representing clients before the Board of Zoning Adjustments.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that the Planning Commission member must disqualify himself from decisions affecting his firm's financial interests, but may work on most projects if he avoids using his official position to influence relevant decisions and properly disqualifies himself when necessary. The analysis distinguishes between decisions where disqualification is required and situations where participation is permitted, with specific guidance on appeals and presentations before bodies outside his direct control.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, old; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (dated November 23, 1983) and the FPPC response advice letter (dated January 6, 1984). The advice letter is comprehensive with five distinct situations analyzed with specific conclusions for each. Letter includes multiple footnotes with references to Government Code sections and enclosed regulatory materials. Minor OCR irregularities noted (e.g., 'oisqualify' for 'disqualify') but do not impair comprehension. Parsed as a single advice letter document due to the continuous nature of the analysis.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does this constitute a conflict of interest due to the fact 
the City of Modesto will be paying the architect who in turn will 
eventually pay me? 
Gordon M. Hart 
Norman E Wilson 
1301 K Street, Modesto, CA 95354 
(209) 521-1 143 


2. The City Council is acting as a Redevelopment Agency for the purpose 
of developing a future $50 million+ convention/community center. 
Although the EIR was reviewed by the Planning Commission, all dec~ 
isions pertaining to location and property acquisition were made 
prior to my appointment. 
A recent study by a consultant suggested a 
negative conclusion concerning potential funding for this project. 
A 
group of local architects, engineers and contractors, including our 
firm, are considering putting together a similar project utilizing 
existing buildings on a similar but identical site thus coming up 
with a considerably less expensive but similarly functioning communi-
ty center. If this potential turns out to be feasible the City will 
be asked to fund the group for these design studies. 
Question: Can our firm participate in this feasibility and design 
study and receive a portion of the fee paid to the group? 
3. As my professional background and experience all involves design and 
construction, the Planning Director and Planning Commission chairman 
desire that I participate in the CIP process next year. 
As you 
probably are aware, deliberations on the CIP only result in a recom-
mendation to the City Council concerning the various items in the 
CIP. 
Question: Does my participation in the hearings and recommendation 
process preclude my office from proposing and/or being hired by the 
City to do architectural or engineering work on any of the CIP pro-
jects eventually adopted by the City Council? 
4. Question: When a client retained my firm to design a project which 
will require zoning or other City planning type approvals, is there 
any prohibition to a representative from my firm, i.e. another archi-
tect or engineer making the presentation to the Commission assuming 
that I step down and do not participate in the decision? 
Correlary Question: Assuming that my associate makes the presentation 
before the Planning Commission (and I have stepped down as noted 
above) and a split vote results and the matter is appealed to the 
City Council, can I personally make this appeal? 
Correlary Question: Do you know of any prohibition that would prevent 
me from personally making zoning or variance type presentations for a 
client before our Board of Zoning Adjustment (BZA)? 
The BZA is 
appointed by the City Council and its decisions are either ratified 
by or can be appealed to the City Council. 


-
I appreciate the opportunity to pose these questions to you and 
apolo~ 
gize for the lengthy dissertation. 
As you can see there is a common 
thread running through all of the questions involving a member of the 
Planning Commission either receiving City funds for City work or making 
presentations for City bodies. 
Thanking you in advance for your consideration.

CONCLUSION: Situation #1: You will be required to disqualify yourself from Planning Commission decisions which will foreseeably have a material financial effect on the contractor. Situation #2: Your firm, as part of a joint venture, may participate in, and be paid for, designing an alternative to the City's planned community center. Situation #3: Participating in CIP preparation will not prevent your firm from later working on projects authorized in the CIP, but you will be prohibited from influencing decisions concerning particular projects if your firm is interested. Situation #4: Your associate is free to make the presentation to the Planning Commission. You may appear on your client's behalf before the City Council if your firm appeals a Planning Commission decision. Situation #5: You are free to represent your clients before the BZA, assuming the Planning Commission does not have budgetary power over the Board.

Your letter described four situations, and you asked how the Act's provisions apply to each: Situation #1: The City Council hired a Southern California architectural firm to provide preliminary studies and, eventually, construction drawings and bid documents for a new police administration facility. embedding.summary: The FPPC provides comprehensive conflict of interest advice to Gordon M. Hart, a Modesto Planning Commission member and 100% owner of an architectural and engineering firm, addressing five specific scenarios involving potential business opportunities with the City. The letter analyzes each situation under Political Reform Act provisions and provides detailed guidance on when disqualification is required and when business activities are permissible.
